title: Heralded Entanglement Transfer from Entangled Atomic Pair to Free Electrons
authors: Du Ran, Reuven Ianconescu, Shuai Liu, Ya-dong Li, Ji-Yuan Bai, Ze-Long He, Zhi-Cheng Shi, Yan Xia, Avraham Gover
abstract: We propose a protocol that transfers entanglement from an entangled atomic two-level-system (TLS) resource to a pair of free electrons in an energy-sideband ladder via local electron-TLS interactions. In a controlled rotating-wave regime, closed-form reduced states are derived. TLS heralding then prepares a maximally entangled electron state in a two-dimensional single-excitation manifold, with a simple dependence on the initial TLS resource entanglement. Numerical integration of the full bilinear Hamiltonian quantifies the impacts of detuning and pulse shaping and identifies the leading beyond-rotating-wave corrections. The results establish a heralded route to entangled free electrons and will facilitate further advances in quantum electron optics.
summary: This paper proposes a method to transfer quantum entanglement from an atomic pair to free electrons. By measuring the atoms after the interaction (heralding), one can certify the creation of an entangled electron state. The study shows that the amount of entanglement transferred depends directly on the initial atomic entanglement. The authors also quantify how much the results deviate from ideal models due to detuning and higher-order physical effects.
main_problem: Developing a protocol to transfer entanglement from an existing entangled atomic two-level-system (TLS) resource to a pair of free electrons in quantum electron optics.
main_result: The protocol achieves heralded entanglement transfer where the final electron entanglement is determined solely by the initial TLS concurrence, following the relation E_AB = log2(1 + C_12).
method: The authors use analytical derivations in the rotating-wave approximation (RWA) and compare them with numerical integration of the full bilinear Hamiltonian to quantify corrections.
model_or_system: The system consists of two spatially separated free electrons interacting with two spatially separated atomic two-level systems (TLS) via Coulombic dipole coupling, modeled using an energy-sideband ladder.
key_observables: TLS concurrence (C_12), electron logarithmic negativity (E_AB^N), heralding probability, and target-manifold weight.
important_parameters: Detuning (delta), pulse area (g), interaction time (T), and the initial TLS entanglement (alpha, beta).
main_assumptions: The primary analytical framework relies on the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA) and a discrete-sideband plane-wave approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) shows the time-resolved dynamics of the decreasing TLS concurrence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the entanglement transfer problem, defines the Hamiltonian and interaction model, derives analytical solutions under RWA, performs numerical benchmarking against the full model, and discusses systematic corrections and future extensions.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical blueprint for generating entanglement in free-electron systems, a key platform for quantum electron optics, and explores the fundamental limits of entanglement transfer via heralded measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Entanglement-transfer dynamics between the TLSs and the free-electrons. (a) Unconditional entanglement mea- sures. Left axis: TLS concurrence C12(t). Right axis: electron logarithmic negativity EAB N (t), shown for the RWA numerics, the full bilinear model (FULL) numerics, and the resonant closed-form RWA benchmark. (b) Heralded extraction into the two-dimensional target manifolds H(±) 2 . Left axis: un- conditional target-manifold weight w(±) 2 (t). Right axis: log- arithmic negativity EAB,(2,±) N (t) of the normalized projected state ˆρ(2,±) AB (t). Parameters: ∆= 0 and pulse area g = π/4.

FIG. 2. Closed-form relation between the postselected elec- tron log-negativity and the initial TLS concurrence. (a) shows EAB,(2,−) N (T) versus C12(0), where the analytic prediction in Eq. (24) (solid) is compared with numerical RWA evolution (dashed). In the symmetric resonant case the (2, +) and (2, −) branches coincide. (b) displays the same quantity as a func- tion of the TLS amplitude α for the initial resource.
